findings.vulnerable_code: query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}"
findings.description: SQL query constructed via f-string with user input from request.args. Attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via ?id=1 OR 1=1.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries: db.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', (user_id,))

findings.vulnerable_code: query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username LIKE '%" + name + "%'"
findings.description: SQL query constructed via string concatenation with user input. Allows full SQL injection.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries with LIKE placeholder.
findings.fixed_code: db.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username LIKE ?', (f'%{name}%',))

findings.description: SQL query built using .format() with user-controlled email parameter.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries.

findings.description: User input directly executed as shell command via os.system(). Attacker can run arbitrary commands.
findings.remediation: Use subprocess.run with explicit argument list, no shell=True.

findings.description: subprocess with shell=True and user input. Allows command injection via ;, |, or &&.
findings.remediation: Remove shell=True, use list arguments.

findings.vulnerable_code: query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '{username}' AND password = '{password}'"
findings.description: Authentication bypass via SQL injection. Attacker can login as any user with username=admin'--.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries AND hash password before comparison.
findings.fixed_code:
db.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ?', (username,))
# Then compare hashed password

findings.description: Database credentials with username and password hardcoded in source code.
findings.remediation: Load from environment variables or secrets manager.

findings.description: Reflected XSS — user input from request.args rendered directly in HTML without escaping.
findings.remediation: Use Flask's auto-escaping or mark as JSON response.

findings.description: Reflected XSS via error message parameter.
findings.remediation: Return JSON error or use Flask's render_template with auto-escaping.

findings.description: User controls filename without validation. Allows reading any file via ../../path traversal.
findings.remediation: Validate filename against allowlist, use os.path.realpath to check within bounds.
findings.fixed_code:
real_path = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(base_dir, filename))
if not real_path.startswith(base_dir): abort(403)

findings.description: Admin endpoint with no authentication or authorization check. Any user can delete other users.
findings.remediation: Add @login_required decorator and role-based access control.

findings.description: Full Python traceback and exception details exposed to client on error.
findings.remediation: Log internally, return generic error message to client.

findings.description: MD5 used for password hashing. MD5 is cryptographically broken and can be brute-forced or reversed.
findings.remediation: Use bcrypt or argon2 for password hashing.

findings.description: Debug mode enabled exposes Werkzeug debugger, stack traces, and allows code execution via debugger console.
findings.remediation: Set DEBUG based on environment variable, default to False.

findings.description: Flask SECRET_KEY hardcoded. Can be used to forge session cookies.
findings.remediation: Load from environment or generate at first run.

findings.description: Session cookies accessible via JavaScript (HttpOnly=False), enabling XSS-based session theft.
findings.remediation: Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = True and SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True.

findings.description: CORS allows any origin to make cross-site requests. Combined with session cookies, this enables CSRF attacks.
findings.remediation: Set explicit allowed origins list.
findings.fixed_code:
ALLOWED_ORIGINS = os.environ.get('ALLOWED_ORIGINS', '').split(',')
if origin in ALLOWED_ORIGINS: response.headers['Access-Control-Allow-Origin'] = origin

findings.description: Using Python's random (non-cryptographic PRNG) for security token generation. Predictable by attacker.
findings.remediation: Use secrets module for cryptographic randomness.

findings.description: Server binds to all interfaces (0.0.0.0), making it accessible from any network interface.
findings.remediation: Bind to 127.0.0.1 in development, use proper reverse proxy in production.

findings.vulnerable_code: db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE username LIKE '%${search}%'`)
findings.description: SQL injection via template literal with user input from request query parameter.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized query with placeholder values.
findings.fixed_code: const [rows] = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username LIKE ?', [`%${search}%`])

findings.vulnerable_code: db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id)
findings.description: SQL injection via string concatenation with URL parameter.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized query.
findings.fixed_code: const [rows] = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id])

findings.description: Command injection via child_process.exec with user-controlled target parameter.
findings.remediation: Use execFile with explicit argument array.
findings.fixed_code:
const { execFile } = require('child_process')
execFile('ping', ['-c', '3', target], callback)

findings.description: eval() executes arbitrary JavaScript from user input. Complete remote code execution.
findings.remediation: Remove eval entirely. Use a math expression parser library instead.
findings.fixed_code:
const math = require('mathjs')
const result = math.evaluate(expression)

findings.description: SQL injection in admin delete endpoint via template literal. Combined with no auth, allows arbitrary data destruction.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized query and add authentication middleware.
findings.fixed_code: await db.query('DELETE FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id])

findings.description: SQL injection in user creation endpoint — attacker can create admin accounts or inject malicious data.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized query.
findings.fixed_code: await db.query('INSERT INTO users (username, email) VALUES (?, ?)', [username, email])

findings.description: Path traversal via user-controlled filename. Can read any file on disk using ../../.
findings.remediation: Resolve real path and verify it's within uploads directory.
findings.fixed_code:
const safePath = path.resolve(__dirname, 'uploads', filename)
if (!safePath.startsWith(path.resolve(__dirname, 'uploads'))) return res.status(403).send('Forbidden')

findings.description: Reflected XSS — user input rendered in HTML template literal without escaping.
findings.remediation: Use a template engine with auto-escaping or send JSON.

findings.description: Server-Side Request Forgery — attacker controls URL, can access internal services (localhost, AWS metadata, etc.).
findings.remediation: Validate URL against allowlist of domains, block internal/private IPs.

findings.description: Admin delete endpoint with no authentication middleware. Any user can delete any other user.
findings.remediation: Add authentication and authorization middleware.

findings.description: Insecure Direct Object Reference — user can access any profile by manipulating userId parameter.
findings.remediation: Verify the requesting user owns this profile (session-based auth check).
findings.fixed_code: // Check req.user.id matches userId or has admin role

findings.description: Debug endpoint exposes entire process.env, including all secrets, API keys, and internal hostnames.
findings.remediation: Remove public debug endpoint entirely, or restrict to admin-only.
findings.fixed_code: // Remove this endpoint entirely in production

findings.description: Database password hardcoded in source code.
findings.remediation: Load from environment variable.

findings.description: API secret key hardcoded in configuration file.
findings.remediation: Load from environment variable or secrets manager.
